title: Preliminaries: Learning Theory & Math Basics
blurb: ★★ High-frequency · The theoretical and mathematical foundations of machine learning: when learning is feasible (Hoeffding, VC dimension), the bias-variance tradeoff and overfitting, plus the linear algebra, calculus and probability tools used throughout the course.

knowledge_points.title: The Learning Problem & Generalization Theory
knowledge_points.recap: Learning replaces an unknown target function $f$ with a hypothesis $g$ chosen from a hypothesis set $H$ using data. Feasibility rests on Hoeffding's inequality, generalized to infinite hypothesis sets via the growth function and VC dimension.

questions.q: Describe the essence of machine learning and name the components of the learning problem.
questions.answer: Machine learning applies when: (1) **a pattern exists**, (2) **we cannot pin it down mathematically**, and (3) **we have data on it**. The components are: the input $x$, the output $y$, an unknown **target function** $f: X \to Y$, a **dataset** $(x_1,y_1),\dots,(x_N,y_N)$, a **hypothesis set** $H$ of candidate formulas, and a **learning algorithm** $A$ that picks the final hypothesis $g \approx f$. Together $H$ and $A$ form the **learning model**.

questions.q: State Hoeffding's inequality and explain what it tells us about learning feasibility.
questions.answer: For a single hypothesis, $P[\,|E_{in}(h) - E_{out}(h)| > \epsilon\,] \le 2e^{-2\epsilon^2 N}$. It says the in-sample error $E_{in}$ (the sample frequency $\nu$) is **probably approximately correct (PAC)**, i.e. likely close to the out-of-sample error $E_{out}$ (the bin frequency $\mu$) for large $N$. The bound is **valid for all $N$ and $\epsilon$** and does **not depend on** $\mu$ or the distribution. This makes learning feasible: with enough data, good in-sample performance generalizes.

questions.q: What is the VC dimension $d_{vc}(H)$ of a hypothesis set?
questions.answer: The VC dimension is the **largest number of points $N$ that $H$ can shatter** (realize all $2^N$ dichotomies). It measures the effective number of parameters / degrees of freedom. A finite $d_{vc}$ guarantees generalization, independent of the learning algorithm, input distribution and target function.

options.text: The largest number of points that $H$ can shatter (produce all $2^N$ dichotomies)
The smallest dataset size that can be shattered
The number of training errors made by $g$

questions.q: Explain the bias-variance decomposition and how model complexity affects each term.
questions.answer: The expected out-of-sample error decomposes as $E_{out} = \text{bias} + \text{variance}$. **Bias** measures how well the average hypothesis $\bar g$ can approximate the target $f$ (approximation error). **Variance** measures how much individual hypotheses vary around $\bar g$ (sensitivity to the particular dataset). A **more complex $H$** lowers bias but raises variance; a **simpler $H$** raises bias but lowers variance. The lesson: match the model complexity to the **data resources, not to the target complexity**.
questions.extend: Complex model: bias down, variance up.

questions.q: Compute the growth functions and break points for positive rays, positive intervals, and convex sets. What is the significance of a break point?
questions.answer: **Positive rays**: $m_H(N) = N+1$, break point $k=2$, $d_{vc}=1$. **Positive intervals**: $m_H(N) = \tfrac{1}{2}N^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}N + 1$, break point $k=3$, $d_{vc}=2$. **Convex sets**: $m_H(N) = 2^N$ (shatters any $N$), break point $k=\infty$, $d_{vc}=\infty$. A **break point** $k$ is a size no dataset of which can be shattered ($m_H(k) < 2^k$). Its key significance: any break point forces $m_H(N)$ to be **polynomial** in $N$, which makes the VC bound useful and learning feasible.
questions.extend: Break point exists $\Rightarrow$ polynomial growth $\Rightarrow$ generalization.

questions.q: A model achieves near-zero training error but high test error. Using the two questions of learning ($E_{out}\approx E_{in}$ and $E_{in}\approx 0$), diagnose the problem.
questions.answer: Learning needs both $E_{out}(g) \approx E_{in}(g)$ (good generalization) **and** $E_{in}(g) \approx 0$ (fits the data). Here $E_{in}\approx 0$ holds but $E_{out} \gg E_{in}$, so the first condition fails. This is **overfitting**: the hypothesis set is too complex (high $d_{vc}$/variance) relative to the amount of data, so the generalization gap $\Omega$ is large. Remedies: simpler $H$, regularization, or more data.

knowledge_points.title: Linear Algebra & Matrix Calculus
knowledge_points.recap: Vectors, matrices and their operations, norms and loss functions, plus scalar/vector derivatives (gradient, Jacobian) and the chain rule form the computational backbone of ML.

questions.q: Define the L1-norm and L2-norm of a vector, and the four properties any norm must satisfy.
questions.answer: For $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$: **L1-norm** $\|x\|_1 = \sum_{i=1}^n |x_i|$; **L2-norm** $\|x\|_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2}$ (note $\|x\|_2^2 = x^Tx$). A norm $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ must satisfy: (1) **non-negativity** $f(x)\ge 0$; (2) **definiteness** $f(x)=0 \iff x=0$; (3) **homogeneity** $f(tx)=|t|f(x)$; (4) **triangle inequality** $f(x+y)\le f(x)+f(y)$.

questions.q: What is the difference between the gradient and the Jacobian matrix? Give the shape of each.
questions.answer: For a **scalar-valued** function $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$, the **gradient** $\nabla_x f(x) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the vector of partial derivatives $\partial f/\partial x_i$ (same shape as the input). For a **vector-valued** function $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$, the **Jacobian** $J_f(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ collects all partials, with $(J_f)_{ij} = \partial f_i/\partial x_j$. The gradient is only defined when the function returns a scalar.
questions.extend: Scalar out: gradient. Vector out: Jacobian.

questions.q: For $f(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}x^TAx + b^Tx$ with symmetric $A$, derive $\nabla_x f(x)$ and $\nabla_x^2 f(x)$.
questions.answer: Using $\partial(b^Tx)/\partial x = b$ and $\partial(x^TAx)/\partial x = 2Ax$ (for symmetric $A$): $$\nabla_x f(x) = Ax + b, \qquad \nabla_x^2 f(x) = A.$$ The gradient of the quadratic term gives $Ax$ (the $\tfrac12$ cancels the factor 2), and the Hessian is the constant matrix $A$.

questions.q: For $f(x) = \|Ax - b\|_2^2$ with $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$, $b \in \mathbb{R}^m$, compute $\nabla_x f(x)$.
questions.answer: Expand $f(x) = (Ax-b)^T(Ax-b) = x^TA^TAx - 2b^TAx + b^Tb$. Applying the quadratic-form rule ($A^TA$ is symmetric): $$\nabla_x f(x) = 2A^TAx - 2A^Tb = 2A^T(Ax - b).$$ This is the gradient used in least-squares / linear regression.

questions.q: State the single-variable chain rule and use it to differentiate $h(x) = \sin(x^3)$.
questions.answer: Chain rule: for $h(x) = f(g(x))$, introduce $u = g(x)$; then $\frac{dh}{dx} = \frac{df}{du}\cdot\frac{du}{dx}$. For $h(x)=\sin(x^3)$, let $u = x^3$, so $\frac{df}{du} = \cos(u)$ and $\frac{du}{dx} = 3x^2$. Therefore $$\frac{dh}{dx} = \cos(u)\cdot 3x^2 = 3x^2\cos(x^3).$$

knowledge_points.title: Probability Theory
knowledge_points.recap: Random variables map outcomes to reals; PMF/PDF/CDF describe their distribution; expectation and variance summarize them. Standard distributions (Bernoulli, Binomial, Uniform, Normal) recur throughout ML.

questions.q: Define expectation and variance of a random variable, and give the computational formula for variance.
questions.answer: **Expectation** is a weighted average of values: discrete $E[X] = \sum_{x} x\,p_X(x)$; continuous $E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x f_X(x)\,dx$. **Variance** measures spread around the mean: $\text{Var}[X] = E[(X - E[X])^2] = E[X^2] - E[X]^2$. Useful properties: expectation is **linear** ($E[aX+bY]=aE[X]+bE[Y]$), and $\text{Var}[aX+b] = a^2\text{Var}[X]$.

questions.q: Compute the mean and variance of a fair six-sided die.
questions.answer: With $p_X(x) = 1/6$ for $x \in \{1,\dots,6\}$: $$E[X] = \sum_{x=1}^6 x\cdot\tfrac16 = 3.5.$$ And $E[X^2] = \sum x^2\cdot\tfrac16 = \tfrac{91}{6} = 15.1\overline{6}$, so $$\text{Var}[X] = E[X^2] - E[X]^2 = 15.1\overline{6} - 12.25 = \tfrac{35}{12} \approx 2.917.$$

questions.q: Which statement about the probability density function (PDF) of a continuous random variable is correct?
questions.answer: The PDF is the derivative of the CDF, $f_X(x) = dF_X(x)/dx$. Its value at a point is **not** a probability; probabilities come from integrating over an interval, and $f_X(x)$ can exceed 1. It must satisfy $f_X(x)\ge 0$ and $\int_{-\infty}^\infty f_X(x)\,dx = 1$.

options.text: $f_X(x)$ is the derivative of the CDF and $f_X(x) \ne P(X=x)$
The CDF is the derivative of the PDF

questions.extend: PDF = CDF'; area, not height, is probability.

questions.q: Let $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ and $Z = aX^2 + bX + c$. Determine $E[Z]$.
questions.answer: By linearity of expectation, $E[Z] = aE[X^2] + bE[X] + c$. Since $\text{Var}[X] = E[X^2] - \mu^2 = \sigma^2$, we have $E[X^2] = \sigma^2 + \mu^2$. Therefore $$E[Z] = a(\sigma^2 + \mu^2) + b\mu + c.$$

questions.q: Give the PMF/PDF, mean and variance of the Bernoulli($p$) and Normal($\mu,\sigma^2$) distributions.
questions.answer: **Bernoulli($p$)** (discrete): $p(x) = p^x(1-p)^{1-x}$ for $x\in\{0,1\}$, with $E[X]=p$ and $\text{Var}[X]=p(1-p)$. **Normal($\mu,\sigma^2$)** (continuous): $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^2}$, with $E[X]=\mu$ and $\text{Var}[X]=\sigma^2$.

knowledge_points.title: 🔮 SS26 Likely Exam Questions (predicted)
knowledge_points.recap: Predicted, exam-style questions in the professor's format — concept-first, plus a Keras code item where relevant. Not from a past paper; use to rehearse likely SS26 topics.

questions.q: (a) Explain the **bias-variance tradeoff**. (b) Your model has **low training error but high test error** — is this a bias or a variance problem, and why? (c) Name one thing you would do to fix it, and one thing you would **not** do, and explain why.
questions.answer:
**(a)** The expected out-of-sample error splits as $E_{out} = \text{bias} + \text{variance}$. **Bias** is how far the *average* hypothesis $\bar g$ is from the target $f$ (approximation error); **variance** is how much individual fits wobble around $\bar g$ because they depend on the particular dataset. A **more complex** model lowers bias but raises variance; a **simpler** one does the reverse.

**(b)** This is a **variance** problem (**overfitting**). Low training error means bias is small — the model *can* fit — but the large gap $E_{out}\gg E_{in}$ means it is over-sensitive to the training sample and fails to generalize.

**(c)** **Would do:** reduce complexity / add **regularization** (or collect more data) to shrink variance. **Would NOT do:** make the model *more* complex (deeper/more parameters) — that lowers bias further but **raises variance**, worsening the exact problem. The lesson: match complexity to the **data resources, not the target's complexity**.
questions.extend: Low $E_{in}$ + big gap = high variance = overfitting; the fix is *less* capacity or more data, not more.

questions.q: (a) Why is a **finite VC dimension** important for learning? (b) The 2D perceptron has $d_{vc}=3$ while convex sets have $d_{vc}=\infty$ — what does this difference tell you about their ability to generalize?
questions.answer:
**(a)** A finite VC dimension guarantees the **growth function $m_H(N)$ is polynomial** in $N$ (once a break point exists), so the VC generalization bound is useful: with enough data, $E_{out}\approx E_{in}$ **regardless of the input distribution, target function, or learning algorithm**. Infinite $d_{vc}$ leaves $m_H(N)=2^N$ and the bound never becomes informative.

**(b)** The perceptron's $d_{vc}=3$ means it has limited, controlled capacity, so a modest amount of data makes it generalize — its in-sample performance transfers to unseen points. **Convex sets** ($d_{vc}=\infty$) can shatter *any* number of points; they can always fit the training data perfectly but **carry no generalization guarantee** — they overfit. More capacity = more data needed to close the generalization gap.
questions.extend: Finite $d_{vc}$ ⇒ polynomial growth ⇒ generalization possible; $d_{vc}=\infty$ ⇒ can memorize, can't be trusted out-of-sample.

questions.q: (a) Why do we split data into **training, validation, and test** sets — what is each used for? (b) You standardize features by fitting the scaler on the *whole* dataset before splitting. Why is this a mistake, and how would you do it correctly?
questions.answer:
**(a)** The **training set** fits the model's parameters; the **validation set** tunes hyperparameters and drives model selection / early stopping (it detects overfitting); the **test set** is touched **only once at the very end** to give an *unbiased* estimate of generalization. If the test set influences any choice, the estimate becomes optimistically biased.

**(b)** Fitting the scaler on all the data lets **test-set statistics (mean/variance) leak into training** — the model indirectly sees the test distribution, so its reported performance is too optimistic. Correct practice: **fit the scaler on the training set only**, then *apply* that fixed transform to the validation and test sets (and refit inside each fold during cross-validation).
questions.extend: Train = learn params · val = tune hyperparams · test = final unbiased score. Always fit preprocessing on train only — no leakage.
